race.name: Trek 100
race.tagline: Annual charity bike ride through Wisconsin's rolling farmland benefiting childhood cancer research.

vitals.location: Trek HQ, 801 West Madison Street, Waterloo, Wisconsin 53594

climate.description: Typical June weather in southern Wisconsin features mild temperatures and variable conditions suitable for cycling through farmland.

terrain.surface: Paved secondary roads through rolling dairy farmland in Jefferson, Columbia, Dane, and Dodge Counties with challenging hillsides and flats; MTB option on private singletrack trails at Trek HQ.
features: Paved secondary roads through rolling dairy farmland in Jefferson, Columbia, Dane, and Dodge Counties with challenging hillsides and flats
MTB option on private singletrack trails at Trek HQ

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 74. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 71.

course_description.character: Rolling paved roads through dairy farmland with quiet secondary roads, challenging hillsides, flats, and fun segments; new loop-style courses; MTB on private moderate singletrack (10mi laps, no jumps).
course_description.signature_challenge: Hilly 100-mile route with 3,889ft elevation through beautiful Wisconsin countryside.

logistics.lodging_strategy: Hotels in Waterloo, Lake Mills, or Madison; camping not specified.
logistics.transport: Fully supported ride with staggered starts (7:30am for 100/62mi, 9:30am for shorter), rest stops, volunteers, and law enforcement at intersections; roads open to traffic; arrive 1hr early; post-ride party.

history.origin_story: Annual charity ride since 1990 benefiting MACC Fund; over $22M raised; 2025 marks Trek's 50th anniversary.
notable_moments: 2025: 50th Trek anniversary; new 25mi loop and MTB course

biased_opinion.summary: The Trek 100 stands out as a feel-good charity powerhouse, blending accessible routes, Trek's polish, and a noble cause—perfect for community-minded riders seeking fun over suffering.

final_verdict.one_liner: Premier charity ride with heart, hills, and headquarters party.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Trek 100 delivers a polished, inclusive cycling experience through Wisconsin's picturesque farmland, raising millions for childhood cancer research while offering diverse distances and a festive vibe at Trek HQ. Ideal for fundraisers who want challenge without cutthroat competition.

youtube_data.search_query: Trek 100 gran fondo cycling Trek HQ

videos.title: BIG GRAVEL TEST FOR THE TREK DOMANE SL7
videos.description:
In this episode we test the Trek Domane 2023 on an adventurous route around the Pennines and test its capabilities on a MTB route around a well know forest.

We cover a total 100k 85% off road on the endurance bike. #trekdomane #bikereview #cyclingadventure 

Social 
Instagram 
https://instagram.com/benjamin_le_chappers

Support 
https://www.teamcycles.com
https://www.sungod.co/en-gb/
https://pillar-app.com/

videos.title: How To: Shift a Road Bike
videos.description:
Shop Road Bikes: http://bit.ly/2zzEo6P

Knowing how to shift the gears on your road bikes helps you ride fast and climb hills with ease.  Keep watching to learn which gears work with which terrain and how and when to change them to keep your ride fast and fun.

Find your local Trek retailer: http://bit.ly/2R2c2IV
videos.transcript:
the left sure knowing how to shift your
gears not only helps you ride faster but
it also makes riding more fun so let's
talk about how shifting works and when
to change gears we'll start by taking a
look at the parts shifters are found on
the front of the handlebars they control
the bikes to ralliers which move the
chain from gear to gear across the drive
train your left shifter controls your
front derailleur which moves the chain
between your chain rings on your crank
set shifting with your left hand will
cause more dramatic changes and is
helpful for riding on Hills by pushing
the whole left hand lever inward the
derailleur will push the chain up to a
harder gear harder gear takes more
effort to pedal but your wheel moves
farther her pedal stroke pushing only
the small inner lever inward will pull
the chain down to an easier gear this
makes pedaling easier but you won't move
as far or as fast your best bet is to
use the smaller chain ring or the
easiest gear when pedaling uphill and
the big ring or your hardest gear when
you're riding downhill
you're right shifter controls the rear
derailleur shifting the chain across
cogs in the cassette in the back the
smaller sized cog is actually the harder
faster gear the bigger cogs make for
easier pedaling but slower speeds this
means your right hand shifter works
backwards of your left hand by pushing
the whole right hand lover inward the
derailleur shifts the chain to an easier
gear and pushing the small inner lever
will shift the chain to a harder gear
and faster speeds compared to front
shifting the difference between gears is
much smaller out back with practice
you'll find that front shifting is
helpful for big changes on hills and
rear shifting is good for fine tuning
until your pedaling at a comfortable
rate there are two gear combinations to
avoid this is when you're in the hardest
gear on one shifter and the easiest in
the other
we call these cross-chaining because
you're running in opposite extremes
cross-chaining puts a lot of strain on
the chain and often causes the chain to
rub
it's the front dérailleur which makes a
lot of irritating noise and can be bad
on your gear so what gear combination is
best it's a matter of personal taste
what we're really after is the most
comfortable and efficient rate of
pedaling or cadence in general most
riders find a cadence of about 90 pedal
rotations per minute to be the most
efficient and comfortable a slower
cadence and feel like a struggle a
faster cadence feels like you're
pedaling like crazy but going nowhere if
you're interested in measuring your
cadence many cycling computers or apps
can read this with a sensor using a
sensor to measure your cadence and be a
great way to learn when it's a good time
to change gears finally you need to keep
pedaling while shifting and it works
best when you're pedaling lightly if
you've got a lot of pressure on the
pedals when shifting the change tends to
be clunky and abrupt if you see a hill
coming up shift do an easier gear before
you start climbing and that's the 101 of
shifting gears if you're totally new to
this it helps to practice shifting in an
empty parking lot if you'd like more
help pedal over to your local Trek
dealer for some personalized advice

videos.title: TREK Bikes and why I’d never buy one.
videos.description:
TREK are too heavy and way too over priced!

I know a guy with a TREK Madone (or Mad-One as I like to call them because you would need to mad to buy one!)  and it cost him $20,000 for the full Dura-Ace Di2 12 speed and Dura-Ace wheels… and its 7.9kg in size XS! Like are you having a laugh? $20k and its almonds 8kg. Madness, total Madness. 🤷🏻‍♂️

Thank you for watching my video. 

If you have any questions just ask in the comments and I will get back to you. 

Please do subscribe,
Like the video,
Send it to a friend, 
And click the Bell 🔔 for more videos. 

Safe riding, 

Reg.

videos.title: Trek Marlin 7 Gen 3 Review: The Best Budget Mountain Bike in 2026?
videos.description:
Links Get From Here:

Trek Marlin 7 Gen 3 Review: The Best Budget Mountain Bike in 2026?

Is the 2026 Trek Marlin 7 Gen 3 still the king of entry-level mountain bikes? In this deep-dive review, we’re putting the newest generation of the Trek Marlin 7 to the test. From the updated trail geometry to the SRAM NX Eagle 12-speed drivetrain, we cover why this bike feels more like a "real" trail machine than ever before.

[Key Features & Tech Specs] The Marlin 7 Gen 3 has moved away from its XC roots toward a more aggressive, stable trail feel. We break down the key specs that matter for 2026:

Frame: Alpha Silver Aluminum with internal routing (including dropper post).

Suspension: RockShox Judy Silver with 100mm of travel and Solo Air spring.

Drivetrain: Reliable SRAM NX Eagle 12-speed for effortless climbing.

Safety: Shimano MT200 hydraulic disc brakes for serious stopping power.

Updates: Dropper post-ready frame and slack 66.5° head tube angle.

[Why This Video is Different] Whether you’re a beginner looking for your first "proper" MTB or a commuter needing a rugged daily driver, this review covers ride quality, weight, and the best accessories to pair with the Marlin 7.

[Timestamps] 0:00 Trek Marlin 7 Gen 3 Overview 1:20 What's New? Gen 2 vs Gen 3 3:15 RockShox Judy Solo Air Test 5:40 SRAM NX Eagle Shifting Performance 8:10 Best Accessories & Upgrades 11:05 Final Verdict: Who is this for?

#TrekMarlin7, #Marlin7Gen3, #TrekBikes, #MTBReview, #MountainBike, #HardtailMTB, #CyclingTech, #TrekMarlin, #BikeReview, #MTBLife 

► Some More Videos You Might Find Interesting ◄

1. Best Floor standing Speakers
https://youtu.be/Q2nwX7wgnw8

2. Best Portable Music Players
https://youtu.be/CiOAmA6c2VA

3. Best Motorcycle Speakers
https://youtu.be/S7X3qbrd7HE

4. Best 17-inch Laptops
https://youtu.be/lnXmQLvT2fg

5. Best Mobile Hotspot
https://youtu.be/hKInl9x2uCs



Thanks for watching guys, I hope you liked this video.


►Disclaimer◄

Some of the footage used in this video is not original content produced by Review Genesis Portions of stock footage of products was gathered from multiple sources including, manufactures, fellow creators and various other sources. If something belongs to you, and you want it to be removed, please do not hesitate to contact us at techteam7712@gmail.com

FTC DISCLOSURE: Review Genesis is a participant in the Amazon Services LLC Associates Program, an affiliate advertising program designed to provide a means for sites to earn advertising fees by advertising and linking to Amazon.com. As an Amazon Associate I earn from qualifying purchases.

This supports the channel to run and adds no extra cost to you as a customer.

#CommissionsEarned
videos.transcript:
Ever felt that pull to just hop on a
bike and disappear down a dirt trail,
letting the city noise fade behind you?
That feeling of freedom, speed, and
control is exactly what the Trek Marlin
7 Gen 3 is built for. Whether you're a
cycle lover craving weekend trail
adventures, a learner stepping into the
world of mountain biking, or a potential
buyer searching for a reliable hard tail
that truly delivers value, this bike
deserves your attention. The TRE Marlin
7 Gen 3 sits comfortably in that sweet
spot between beginnerfriendly design and
performance ready features. TRE has
refined this generation with trail
confidence in mind, making it more
capable than earlier versions while
still keeping it approachable. At first
glance, the clean frame design and
modern geometry immediately suggest a
bike that's ready for real riding, not
just casual spins around the
neighborhood. [music] It looks sporty
without being intimidating, which is a
big win for riders who are still
building confidence. One of the standout
features of the Trek Marlin 7 Gen 3 is
its updated frame geometry. The slightly
slacker head angle and longer reach
improve stability on descents and make
the bike feel planted on rough terrain.
This means better control when trails
get technical, yet it still feels agile
enough for everyday riding. For
learners, this geometry helps build
confidence while experienced cyclists
will appreciate how composed the bike
feels when the trail gets unpredictable.
Another strong point is the SR Sunour
air suspension fork. Compared to basic
coil forks, an air fork is lighter, more
adjustable, and more responsive. On the
Marlin 7 Gen 3, it does a solid job of
soaking up bumps, roots, and small
rocks, giving you a smoother and more
comfortable ride. This is especially
important for new riders who might feel
overwhelmed by trail chatter. The
suspension helps reduce fatigue and
makes longer rides more enjoyable. When
it comes to shifting, Tre equips this
bike with a oneby drivetrain, which is a
big upgrade for simplicity and
reliability. With a wide range cassette,
you get plenty of gears for climbing
hills and maintaining speed on flat
sections without the confusion of a
front derailure. For learners, this
makes riding more intuitive. And for
experienced cyclists, it means less
maintenance and cleaner performance.
Braking is another area where the Trek
Marlin 7 Gen 3 shines. The hydraulic
disc brakes provide consistent stopping
power in both dry and wet conditions.
This is crucial for trail riding where
quick and reliable braking can make a
huge difference in safety and
confidence. Even on steep descents, the
brakes feel controlled rather than
grabby, which is ideal for riders still
developing their skills. Now, let's
clearly break down the pros. First, the
modern trail focused geometry offers
excellent stability and confidence,
especially for off-road riding. Second,
the air suspension fork provides better
comfort and adjustability compared to
entry-level coil forks. Third, the one
by drivetrain simplifies gear shifting
and reduces maintenance while still
offering a wide gear range. Fourth, the
hydraulic disc brakes deliver strong,
reliable stopping power in all weather
conditions. Of course, no bike is
perfect, and it's important to be honest
about the cons. One downside is that the
Marlin 7 Gen 3 is still a hard tail, so
riders looking for plush comfort on very
rough or aggressive trails might miss
rear suspension. Another con is that
while it's trail capable, it's not
designed for extreme downhill or
advanced enduro riding, which could
limit highly aggressive riders. So, who
is the Trek Marlin 7 Gen 3 really for?
It's ideal for beginners who want a bike
they won't outgrow too quickly, cycle
lovers who enjoy mixed terrain, and
buyers looking for a trustworthy brand
with solid components. It handles daily
rides, fitness cycling, and trail
adventures with impressive balance. To
wrap things up, the Trek Marlin 7 Gen 3
is a smart, well-rounded mountain bike
that blends comfort, control, and
reliability in a way few bikes at this
level manage to achieve. It doesn't try
to be something it's not, but what it
does, it does very well. If you're
searching for a bike that can grow with
your skills and keep your riding
exciting, the Trek Marlin 7 Gen 3 is
[music] definitely worth a closer look.

videos.title: Lizzie Deignan's Trek Emonda SLR | The Queen Of Paris-Roubaix's Pro Bike
videos.description:
The first women’s winner of Paris-Roubaix, Lizzie Deignan, is one of the all time cycling greats. She's a former world champion, a winner of La Course, the Tour of Flanders and Liège-Bastogne-Liège, among many other top races. With Lizzie currently in Britain racing the Women’s Tour for Trek-Segafredo, we were lucky enough to get our hands on her beautiful Trek Emonda SLR for our latest pro bike check!

Bike Specs: 

Frame and forks: Trek Emonda SLR 800 series OCLV Carbon
Wheels: Bontrager Aeolus RSL37 Disc Tubular
Tyres: Pirelli P Zero Tubular SL (with TPU thermoplastic tubes saving 50g per tyre)
Handlebars: Bontrager Aeolus RSL
Saddle: Bontrager Ajna White (144mm wide)
Gearbox: 12-speed SRAM RED eTap AXS (with wireless shifting)
Brakes: SRAM 160mm (front) / 140mm (rear) disc rotors
Chainset: SRAM Red 50/37T chainrings
Pedals: Shimano Dura-Ace R9100
Powermeter: Quarq (incorporated with chainrings)
Cassette: 10/33T XD-R
Head unit: Wahoo Elemnt Roam with K-Edge Out Front Alloy Mount

Bike weight: 6.90kg
Saddle height: 68.5cm
Reach: 55cm
Bar width: 380mm
Stem length: 120mm
Crank length: 170mm

What do you make of Lizzie’s Trek Emonda SLR? Let us know in the comments below! 👇

this is the trekkermonda slr as ridden

it's designed as trek's lightweight
racing do it all bike it's constructed

colorway really do like that especially
the design intricacy at the front of the

rsl37
and these are the tubular versions
paired to pirelli p0 tubular tyres these
are in fact the sl version with the
lighter inner tube in there so the
energy inside these tubulars is the tpu
super lightweight version to reduce

at the center of the wheels we've got
the disc brake rotors 160 at the front
and 140 at the rear the super cool thing
about the paintwork on this bike is it's

just one layer thick and in the sunlight
you can in fact see through the paint
all the way down to the carbon layup and
it looks super cool
the bike is built up using sram's red

the bike we've got the red chain set
which is using 170 millimeter long

rings which also incorporates the cork

this chain set is that is machined from
one single piece which reduces the
weight and increases the strength
attached to the crank arms are shimano
dura ace pedals and also just tucks
behind the chain set is a neat little

at the rear of the bike is the cassette
this is a 10 all the way up to a 33
tooth providing a really wide range of

terrains moving forwards and up on the
bike we have that trek bontrager ajna
saddle now an interesting point here is
that lizzy actually beds a lot of these

rides each one for about a month or so
on her training bike to make sure they
all gonna feel exactly the same
regardless of whether she gets on her
race bike her spare bike or her training
bike we can see here this saddle is
white and this dictates the fact that it
is in fact her race bike it's 144
millimeters wide it has the seven by
nine mil carbon rails to keep the weight
down and make it as lightweight as
possible and in the center here we've
got a large central cut out to help keep
lizzy comfy on the bike and an
interesting point also is that the
mechanics have actually put little tip x
marks on the rails and on the seat post
itself to dictate exactly where it is so
there's no risk of anything moving and
they can get the position set up just
right one of the interesting parts about
the amanda frame set is that it has a
completely different system at the seat
tube area so the seat tube extends
further up from the frame and then the
seat post itself sits over the top and
is in fact called a seat mast something
also really cool at the rear here is we
can see lizzy's number holder this is a
special design purely for the armonda
frame sets which trek supply to the race
teams it has a small little number hold
amount bonded onto the frame in place
before the frame is painted and this is
completely different to anything you
ever see on a production model for
example
moving to the front of the bike we have
this super cool one-piece bontrager rsl
carbon cockpit it's got 120 millimeter
long stem which is negative seven
degrees and 38 centimeter wide bars and
we've got this super super fresh white
bar tape again like i said dictating the
fact that this is lizzy's race bike an
interesting fact about these handlebars
is that they have semi integrated cable
routing for the brake hoses this enables
them to be super hidden and smooth the
airflow to make the bike as aerodynamic
as possible but it also makes the
mechanics life much easier should they
need to replace or service any of the
components the brake hoses themselves
route then internally to the frame set
using this specially designed top spacer
on the top of the headset and a really
cool fact i spotted is just tucked at
the back here are the words go go on the
back i really like that it's a nice
little touch finishing this bike off is
a k-edge alloy out the front mount to
hold lizzie's wahoo head unit and
display all the stats and data that she
requires when she's racing and also
we've got some bontrager xxx light
bottle cages these weigh just 20 grams
are now constructed from the same oclv
carbon as what the frame is made out of
a couple of cool little details which
the mechanics have told me about is how
they glue the tubular ties on so they
just apply one coat of glue to the rim a
coat of glue to the tubular and then one
single coat to stick it all together
this secures the tyre to the wheel as
best as possible but also reduces the
need for any unnecessary glue to reduce
the weight as well an interesting point
here at the seat mast is the small trek
segafredo sticker which the mechanics
use to designate the height of the
saddle on each race bike
so i've had the gzn tape measure of

a saddle height of 68.5 centimeters and
the saddle to handlebar drop is 6.5
centimeters now this bike feels
incredibly light but how much does it
weigh on the gcn scales let me just zero
these
oh
6.9 kilograms
super light right next up let's get this

oh lovely
so there you have it the bike of lizzie
diagon hope you enjoyed taking a closer

quotes.text: 100K just over 100k planned... it's taking us up the waskely way in the northeast of England around the top of the pennines and then making its way down towards hamsley Forest... this route has absolutely everything Rivers mountain bike trails Hills.

I'm on my Trek domane and I've set it up on the Hutchinson Tundras sort of a 40 inch mountain bike tire... the clearance is almost minimal and it was a bit hit or miss but it's worked these are Hutchinson Tundra 40s and I think that's the most you're going to get on the trek domane.

gear_mentions.text: 8.8 kg carbon fiber bike with electronic SRAM Rival eTap AXS system and carbon rims costs nearly $7,000.
Trek's Domane with ISOSpeed decouplers makes the bike more comfortable and practical, weighs 10.28 kg for around $5,000.
Pirelli P Zero tubular tires with TPU super lightweight inner tubes to reduce weight and rolling resistance.
SRAM Red eTap AXS group set with 50/37 tooth chainrings and 10-33 tooth cassette provides wide range for different terrains.

race_day_tips.text: If you see a hill coming up, shift to an easier gear before you start climbing.
Keep pedaling while shifting and it works best when you're pedaling lightly - avoid putting a lot of pressure on the pedals when shifting.
Aim for about 90 pedal rotations per minute as most riders find this cadence to be the most efficient and comfortable.

additional_quotes.text: Lizzy actually beds a lot of these saddles in pre-season and then hands them all across to the mechanics so she rides each one for about a month or so on her training bike to make sure they all gonna feel exactly the same regardless of whether she gets on her race bike her spare bike or her training bike.

rider_intel.search_text: The Trek 100 race transcripts focus heavily on bike setup and gear considerations rather than course-specific details. Riders discuss the importance of proper shifting technique, particularly shifting to easier gears before climbing hills and maintaining light pedal pressure during shifts. A comfortable cadence of around 90 RPM is recommended as most efficient. Equipment discussions cover high-end carbon bikes weighing 8.8-10.28 kg with electronic shifting systems like SRAM Red eTap AXS. Tubular tires with lightweight TPU inner tubes are mentioned for reduced rolling resistance. The Trek Domane with ISOSpeed decouplers is highlighted for comfort on longer rides. Pro mechanics bed in multiple saddles during pre-season to ensure consistent feel across race and training bikes. Wide-range cassettes like 10-33 tooth provide versatility for varying terrain demands typical of gravel racing.
